subtopics: Covariance $\cdot$ Correlation $\cdot$ PSD Matrices $\cdot$ Portfolio Theory $\cdot$ Order Statistics
prerequisites: Topic 4 — Gaussian Distributions; Topic 5 — Expectation & Variance
learningObjectives: **Compute covariance and correlation from first principles** using $\text{Cov}(X,Y)=\mathbb{E}[XY]-\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ and the bilinearity of covariance, including cases involving Multinomial components.
**Construct correlated normal random variables** via the decomposition $Y=\rho X+\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}\,Z$ and generalise to $n$ correlated normals using the Cholesky factorisation.
**Understand what makes a covariance matrix valid** — the positive semi-definiteness (PSD) condition — and use it to derive feasibility constraints on correlation triples.

**Identify the equicorrelation lower bound** $\rho\geq -1/(n-1)$ for $n$ variables with all pairwise correlations equal to $\rho$, and construct an explicit model achieving any $\rho$ in the valid range.
**Apply covariance to portfolio construction** — derive the optimal portfolio weights for maximum Sharpe ratio and interpret the role of correlation in diversification.
**Explain the time-scale correlation paradox** — how the sign of correlation between two assets can depend on the measurement frequency.

**Compute the covariance of order statistics** for i.i.d.\
 Uniform$(0,1)$ samples, using the joint distribution of extremes.

blocks.title: Core Definitions and Properties
blocks.content:
**Covariance:** $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] = \mathbb{E}[(X-\mathbb{E}[X])(Y-\mathbb{E}[Y])]$.

**Correlation:** $\text{Corr}(X,Y) = \rho_{XY}= \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\text{Var}(Y)}}\in [-1,1]$.

**Bilinearity of Covariance:** $\text{Cov}(aX+bY, Z) = a\text{Cov}(X,Z)+b\text{Cov}(Y,Z)$.

$\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X)+\text{Var}(Y)+2\text{Cov}(X,Y)$.

$\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i} a_{i} X_{i}\right) = \sum_{i} a_{i}^{2}\text{Var}(X_{i}) + 2\sum_{i<j}a_{i} a_{j}\text{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})$.

**Cauchy–Schwarz:** $|\text{Cov}(X,Y)|\leq\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\text{Var}(Y)}$, so $|\rho_{XY}|\leq1$.

**$\rho=\pm1$:** $Y=aX+b$ almost surely for some $a\neq0$.

blocks.title: Covariance Matrix and Positive Semi-Definiteness
blocks.content:
For a random vector $(X_{1},…,X_{n})^{\top}$, the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ has entries $\Sigma_{ij}=\text{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})$. The correlation matrix $C$ has $C_{ij}=\text{Corr}(X_{i},X_{j})$ (diagonal entries all 1).

A covariance or correlation matrix **must be positive semi-definite (PSD)**: $\mathbf{v}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{v}\geq0$ for all $\mathbf{v}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$.

Equivalently, all eigenvalues are $\geq0$.

**Test in 2D:** $\begin{pmatrix}1&\rho\\\rho&1\end{pmatrix}$ is PSD iff $|\rho|\leq1$.

**Test in 3D:** $\begin{pmatrix}1&a&c\\a&1&b\\c&b&1\end{pmatrix}$ is PSD iff $1+2abc - a^{2}-b^{2}-c^{2}\geq0$.

**Covariance of Face Counts in Repeated Dice Rolls.** Roll a fair die 5 times. Let $X$ = number of rolls equal to 1 and $Y$ = number of rolls equal to 2. Find $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$ and $\text{Corr}(X,Y)$.
blocks.solution:
**Distributions.** $(X,Y,…)$ follows a $\mathrm{Multinomial}(5;\, \tfrac{1}{6},\tfrac{1}{6},…)$ distribution. $X\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(5,\tfrac{1}{6})$ and $Y\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(5,\tfrac{1}{6})$ marginally.

**Means and variances:** $\mathbb{E}[X]=\mathbb{E}[Y]=5\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{5}{6}$. $\text{Var}(X)=\text{Var}(Y)=5\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}\cdot\tfrac{5}{6}=\tfrac{25}{36}$.

**$\mathbb{E}[XY]$ via indicator variables.** Write $X=\sum_{i=1}^{5} I_{i}$ and $Y=\sum_{j=1}^{5} J_{j}$ where $I_{i}=\mathbf{1}[\text{roll }i=1]$ and $J_{j}=\mathbf{1}[\text{roll }j=2]$. \[\mathbb{E}[XY] = \sum_{i=1}^{5}\sum_{j=1}^{5} \mathbb{E}[I_{i} J_{j}]\] For $i=j$: $\mathbb{E}[I_{i} J_{i}]=\mathbb{P}(\text{roll }i=1\text{ and }=2)=0$.

For $i\neq j$: $\mathbb{E}[I_{i} J_{j}]=\mathbb{P}(\text{roll }i=1)\cdot \mathbb{P}(\text{roll }j=2)=\tfrac{1}{6}\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{1}{36}$.

There are $5^{2}-5=20$ pairs with $i\neq j$, so: $\mathbb{E}[XY] = 20\cdot\tfrac{1}{36}= \tfrac{20}{36}$.

**Covariance:** \[\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY]-\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] = \frac{20}{36}- \frac{5}{6}\cdot\frac{5}{6}= \frac{20}{36}- \frac{25}{36}= -\frac{5}{36}\]

**Correlation:** \[\text{Corr}(X,Y) = \frac{-5/36}{25/36}= -\frac{1}{5}\]

**Multinomial shortcut.** For $\mathrm{Multinomial}(n;p_{1},…,p_{k})$, $\text{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})=-np_{i}p_{j}$ for $i\neq j$. Here: $-5\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}\cdot \tfrac{1}{6}=-\tfrac{5}{36}$. ✓

**Intuition:** More 1s means fewer 2s (they compete for the same 5 rolls), so the covariance is *negative*.

**Key takeaway:** For Multinomial components, $\text{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})=-np_{i}p_{j}$ always negative. Decompose into indicators to derive it from scratch.

blocks.content: **Constructing Correlated Normal Random Variables.** Construct a normal $X$ and a second normal $Y$ such that $\text{Corr}(X,Y)=\rho$ for a specified $\rho\in[-1,1]$. How does this generalise to a vector of $n$ correlated normals?
blocks.solution:
**Two-variable construction.** Let $X\sim N(0,1)$ and $Z\sim N(0,1)$ with $X\perp\!\!\!\perp Z$. Define: \[Y = \rho X + \sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}\,Z\] Then: $Y\sim N(0,1)$ (since it is a linear combination of independent normals with coefficients summing to 1 in squared terms), $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \rho\text{Cov}(X,X)+\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}\text{Cov}(X,Z) = \rho\cdot1+0=\rho$, and $\text{Corr}(X,Y)=\rho$. ✓

**$n$-variable generalisation via Cholesky decomposition.** Given a target correlation matrix $C$ (which must be PSD), factorise: $C = LL^{\top}$ where $L$ is lower-triangular (Cholesky factor).

Then if $\mathbf{Z}=(Z_{1},…,Z_{n})^{\top}$ with $Z_{i}\stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}N(0,1)$, the vector $\mathbf{X}=L\mathbf{Z}$ has covariance matrix $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}]=L\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{Z}^{\top}]L^{\top}=LIL^{\top}=C$.

**Example: $n=2$.** $C=\begin{pmatrix}1&\rho\\\rho&1\end{pmatrix}$. Cholesky: $L=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\\rho&\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}\end{pmatrix}$. Then $X_{1}=Z_{1}$ and $X_{2}=\rho Z_{1}+\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}Z_{2}$. Matches the two-variable formula above. ✓

**Key takeaway:** The decomposition $Y=\rho X+\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}Z$ is the two-variable Cholesky. For $n$ variables, Cholesky of the correlation matrix produces the construction. This is the standard method for Monte Carlo simulation of correlated assets.

blocks.content: **Time-Scale Dependent Correlation Paradox.** Can two stocks be positively correlated day-by-day yet negatively correlated over a year? Provide a concrete example or proof.
blocks.solution:
**Yes.** This is possible and is a real phenomenon in financial markets (it relates to the difference between short-run co-movement and long-run mean-reversion).

**Concrete construction.** Let $\varepsilon_{t}, \eta_{t} \stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}N(0,1)$ for $t=1,…,T$ and let $\mu_{t}$ be a common factor. Define: \[X_{t} = \mu_{t} + \varepsilon_{t}, \qquad Y_{t} = -\mu_{t} + \eta_{t} + cX_{t-1}\] with a suitable choice of $c$. A simpler example:

Let $r_{X,t}=Z_{t}+\varepsilon_{t}$ and $r_{Y,t}=Z_{t}+\eta_{t}$ be daily returns of stocks $X$ and $Y$, where $Z_{t}$ is a common daily factor and $\varepsilon_{t},\eta_{t}$ are independent noise. Clearly the *daily* correlation is positive: $\text{Corr}(r_{X,t},r_{Y,t})=\text{Var}(Z_{t})/ (\text{Var}(Z_{t})+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2})>0$.

Now suppose the log-price of $X$ is mean-reverting to the log-price of $Y$ over long horizons: \[P_{X,T}= \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_{X,t},\quad P_{Y,T}= \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_{Y,t}\] Add a long-run correction term: $r_{X,t}= Z_{t} + \varepsilon_{t} - \alpha(P_{X,t-1}-P_{Y,t-1})$ with $\alpha>0$. This creates mean-reversion (when $X$ gets far above $Y$ it tends to fall back), making annual cumulative returns *negatively* correlated even though daily returns are positively correlated.

**Minimal clean example.** Two-period model: Day 1: $(X_{1},Y_{1})$ have correlation $\rho_{1}>0$ (positive shock). Day 2: each corrects in the *opposite* direction with magnitude $M$. If $M$ is large enough, the annual cumulative returns $(X_{1}+X_{2}, Y_{1}+Y_{2})$ become negatively correlated.

**Key takeaway:** Correlation depends on the time horizon. Positive short-run co-movement can be dominated by long-run mean reversion, flipping the sign of annual correlation. This is the basis of *pairs trading*: two stocks that positively co-move daily but mean-revert annually can be traded on their annual spread.

blocks.content:
**Correlation Bounds for Three Random Variables.** Let $X,Y,Z$ be random variables with $\text{Corr}(X,Y)=a$, $\text{Corr}(Y,Z)=b$, $\text{Corr}(X,Z)=c$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item Derive the constraint $(a,b,c)$ must satisfy for the correlation matrix to be positive semi-definite.

\item Suppose $a=b=0.9$. What is the range of possible values for $c$?\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) PSD constraint.**

The correlation matrix is: $C = \begin{pmatrix}1&a&c\\a&1&b\\c&b&1\end{pmatrix}$.

For $C$ to be PSD, all principal minors must be $\geq0$ (Sylvester's criterion): \begin{itemize}\item $1\times1$: $1\geq0$. ✓

\item $2\times2$: $1-a^{2}\geq0$ (and similarly for other pairs). Requires $|a|,|b|,|c|\leq1$. ✓

\item $3\times3$ (determinant $\geq0$):\end{itemize} \[\det(C) = 1+2abc - a^{2}-b^{2}-c^{2} \geq 0\]

**The PSD constraint:** \[\boxed{a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - 2abc \leq 1}\] with all pairwise $|a|,|b|,|c|\leq1$.

This defines an ellipsoidal region in $(a,b,c)$-space.

**(b) Range of $c$ when $a=b=0.9$.**

Substitute into $\det(C)\geq0$: \[1 + 2(0.9)(0.9)c - (0.9)^{2} - (0.9)^{2} - c^{2} \geq 0\] \[1 + 1.62c - 0.81 - 0.81 - c^{2} \geq 0\] \[-c^{2} + 1.62c - 0.62 \geq 0\] \[c^{2} - 1.62c + 0.62 \leq 0\] Discriminant: $1.62^{2} - 4(0.62) = 2.6244 - 2.48 = 0.1444$. $\sqrt{0.1444}=0.38$. \[c \in \left[\frac{1.62-0.38}{2},\, \frac{1.62+0.38}{2}\right] = [0.62,\, 1.00]\]

**Interpretation:** If $X,Y$ and $Y,Z$ are each 90\% correlated, then $X,Z$ must also be at least 62\% correlated — transitivity forces high correlation. The intuition: if $X\approx Y$ and $Y\approx Z$, then $X\approx Z$.

**Key takeaway:** The PSD constraint $\det(C)\geq0$ translates to $c\in[ab-\sqrt{(1-a^{2})(1-b^{2})},\; ab+\sqrt{(1-a^{2})(1-b^{2})}]$ in general. High pairwise correlations constrain the third correlation tightly.

blocks.content: **Maximum Sharpe Ratio Portfolio Construction.** Two assets $A$ and $B$: $\mu_{A}=1\%$, $\mu_{B}=2\%$, $\sigma_{A}=\sigma_{B}=1\%$, correlation $\rho=0.5$. Construct a portfolio $w=(w_{A},1-w_{A})$ maximising the Sharpe ratio (assume zero risk-free rate).
blocks.solution:
**Portfolio return and variance.** For weights $(w, 1-w)$: \[\mu_{P} = w\mu_{A} + (1-w)\mu_{B} = w\cdot1\% + (1-w)\cdot2\% = 2\% - w\cdot1\%\] \[\sigma_{P}^{2} = w^{2}\sigma_{A}^{2} + (1-w)^{2}\sigma_{B}^{2} + 2w(1-w)\rho\sigma_{A}\sigma_{B}\] \[= w^{2} + (1-w)^{2} + 2w(1-w)(0.5) \quad (\text{in units of }\%^{2})\] \[= w^{2} + 1 - 2w + w^{2} + w(1-w) = 1 - w + w^{2} \cdot(2-1) \cdots\] More carefully: $\sigma_{P}^{2} = w^{2} + (1-w)^{2} + w(1-w) = w^{2} + 1 - 2w + w^{2} + w - w^{2} = w^{2} - w + 1$.

**Sharpe ratio:** $S(w) = \mu_{P}/\sigma_{P} = (2-w)/\sqrt{w^{2}-w+1}$ (in \%).

**Maximise:** Set $dS/dw=0$. Differentiating and setting numerator to zero: \[\frac{d}{dw}\left[\frac{2-w}{\sqrt{w^{2}-w+1}}\right] = \frac{-\sqrt{w^{2}-w+1}-(2-w)\cdot\frac{2w-1}{2\sqrt{w^{2}-w+1}}}{w^{2}-w+1}= 0\] Numerator = 0: $-(w^{2}-w+1)\cdot2 - (2-w)(2w-1)=0$. $-2w^{2}+2w-2 - (4w-2-2w^{2}+w)=0$. $-2w^{2}+2w-2 - 3w+2+2w^{2}=0$. $-w=0\Rightarrow w=0$.

Hmm, $w=0$ gives $S=2/1=2$ at 100\% B. Check $w=1$: $S=1/1=1$. Check $w=-1$ (short A): $S=3/\sqrt{3}\approx1.73$.

The maximum Sharpe ratio is at $w=0$, i.e., **100\% in asset B**.

**Intuition:** Asset $B$ has the higher expected return and the same volatility. With positive correlation, there is no diversification benefit that outweighs simply holding the better asset.

**When does diversification help?** Diversification improves the Sharpe ratio only when $(\mu_{A}/\sigma_{A}^{2})\neq(\mu_{B}/\sigma_{B}^{2})$ *and* the correlation is sufficiently low. In this problem both have $\sigma=1\%$ but $\mu_{B}>\mu_{A}$, so $B$ dominates on Sharpe.

**Key takeaway:** The tangency (max Sharpe) portfolio weight in asset $A$ is: \[w_{A}^{*} = \frac{(\mu_{A}\sigma_{B}^{2} - \mu_{B}\rho\sigma_{A}\sigma_{B})}{(\mu_{A}\sigma_{B}^{2} + \mu_{B}\sigma_{A}^{2} - (\mu_{A}+\mu_{B})\rho\sigma_{A}\sigma_{B})}\] When $\sigma_{A}=\sigma_{B}$: $w_{A}^{*}=(\mu_{A}-\mu_{B}\rho)/ ((\mu_{A}+\mu_{B})(1-\rho))$. Plugging in: $w_{A}^{*}=(1-0.5\cdot2)/(3\cdot0.5)=0$. ✓

**(a) Feasibility bounds for $n=3$.**

The equicorrelation matrix is: $C = (1-\rho)I + \rho\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$.

Its eigenvalues are $1-\rho$ (with multiplicity $n-1$) and $1+(n-1)\rho$ (with multiplicity 1). For PSD we need all eigenvalues $\geq0$: \[1-\rho\geq0 \implies \rho\leq1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad 1+2\rho\geq0 \implies \rho\geq-\tfrac{1}{2}\] So for $n=3$: $\boxed{\rho\in[-1/2,\,1]}$.

**(b) Construction for any valid $\rho$.**

Let $F\sim N(0,1)$ be a common factor and $\varepsilon_{X},\varepsilon_{Y}, \varepsilon_{Z}\stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}N(0,1)$ independent of $F$. Define: \[X = \sqrt{\rho}\,F + \sqrt{1-\rho}\,\varepsilon_{X} \quad ({\rho\geq0})\] Then $\text{Var}(X)=\rho+(1-\rho)=1$ and $\text{Cov}(X,Y)=\rho\text{Var}(F)=\rho$. So $\text{Corr}(X,Y)=\rho$. By symmetry, all three pairwise correlations equal $\rho$.

For $\rho<0$ (down to $-1/2$): use $\sqrt{|\rho|}$ with opposite sign.

**Explicit formula:** $X_{i} = \sqrt{\rho}\,F + \sqrt{1-\rho}\,\varepsilon_{i}$ when $\rho\geq0$.

For $\rho\in(-1/(n-1),0)$, a slightly modified construction with multiple factors achieves the target.

**(c) Generalisation to $n$ variables.**

For $n$ equicorrelated variables, the PSD condition on the equicorrelation matrix gives: \[\boxed{\rho \in \left[-\frac{1}{n-1},\; 1\right]}\] As $n\to\infty$, the lower bound $\to0$: with many variables all mutually negatively correlated, the constraint becomes increasingly tight (you cannot have all pairs strongly negatively correlated).

The one-factor construction $X_{i}=\sqrt{\rho}F+\sqrt{1-\rho}\varepsilon_{i}$ achieves any $\rho\in[0,1]$. For negative $\rho$ down to $-1/(n-1)$, a direct construction uses: $X_{i} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{n-1}}F + \sqrt{1+\frac{1}{n-1}}\varepsilon_{i}$ with appropriate scaling.

**Key takeaway:** The equicorrelation lower bound $-1/(n-1)$ is one of the most important constraints in multi-asset correlation modelling. As $n$ grows, correlations between many assets must approach zero from below.

**(a)** From Topic 3, $\mathbb{E}[X_{(k)}]=k/(n+1)$.

Proof: the joint PDF of $(X_{(1)},…,X_{(n)})$ is $n!$ on the simplex $0<x_{1}<\cdots<x_{n}<1$. The marginal of $X_{(k)}$ is: $f_{X_{(k)}}(x)=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!}x^{k-1}(1-x)^{n-k}$, a $\mathrm{Beta}(k,n-k+1)$ distribution. $\mathbb{E}[X_{(k)}]=k/(n+1)$. ✓

**(b)** We need $\text{Cov}(X_{(1)},X_{(n)})=\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)}X_{(n)}] -\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)}]\mathbb{E}[X_{(n)}]$.

$\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)}]=1/(n+1)$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_{(n)}]=n/(n+1)$.

**Joint PDF of $(X_{(1)**,X_{(n)})$.} For $0<u<v<1$: \[f_{X_{(1)},X_{(n)}}(u,v) = n(n-1)(v-u)^{n-2}\] (Choose which observation is min: $n$ ways; which is max: $n-1$ ways; the remaining $n-2$ observations lie uniformly in $(u,v)$: density $(v-u)^{n-2}$.)

**Compute $\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)**X_{(n)}]$:} \[\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)}X_{(n)}] = n(n-1)\int_{0}^{1}\int_{0}^{v} uv(v-u)^{n-2}\,du\,dv\] Let $v$ be fixed, substitute $u=vt$ ($t\in[0,1]$, $du=v\,dt$): \[= n(n-1)\int_{0}^{1} v\cdot v^{n}\int_{0}^{1} t(1-t)^{n-2}\,dt\,dv = n(n-1)\int_{0}^{1} v^{n+1}\,dv\cdot\mathrm{B}(2,n-1)\] where $\mathrm{B}(2,n-1)=1!/(n-1)!/(n+1-1+1)…$ More carefully: $\int_{0}^{1} t(1-t)^{n-2}dt = \mathrm{B}(2,n-1)= \frac{1!(n-2)!}{n!}=\frac{1}{n(n-1)}$.

And $\int_{0}^{1} v^{n+1}dv=1/(n+2)$. \[\mathbb{E}[X_{(1)}X_{(n)}] = n(n-1)\cdot\frac{1}{n+2}\cdot\frac{1}{n(n-1)}= \frac{1}{n+2}\]

**Covariance:** \[\text{Cov}(X_{(1)},X_{(n)}) = \frac{1}{n+2}- \frac{1}{n+1}\cdot\frac{n}{n+1}= \frac{1}{n+2}- \frac{n}{(n+1)^{2}}\] \[= \frac{(n+1)^{2} - n(n+2)}{(n+2)(n+1)^{2}}= \frac{n^{2}+2n+1-n^{2}-2n}{(n+2)(n+1)^{2}}= \frac{1}{(n+1)^{2}(n+2)}\]

\[\boxed{\text{Cov}(X_{(1)},X_{(n)}) = \frac{1}{(n+1)^{2}(n+2)}}\]

**(c)** The covariance is *positive*.

**Intuition:** If the minimum $X_{(1)}$ is large, it pushes all observations upward, including the maximum $X_{(n)}$. Similarly, a small minimum does not prevent the maximum from being large, but a large minimum forces the maximum to be even larger. The two extremes co-vary positively because they are both constrained by the same sample of observations.

**Key takeaway:** Order statistics of uniform i.i.d.\ data are *positively correlated*, even the most extreme ones. The correlation is: $\text{Corr}(X_{(1)},X_{(n)})=\frac{1/[(n+1)^{2}(n+2)]}{1/[(n+1)^{2}(n+2)]\cdot…}$. For $n=2$: $\text{Cov}=1/12$, $\text{Var}(X_{(1)})=\text{Var}(X_{(2)})=1/12$, so $\text{Corr}=1/2$.

blocks.content:
**Topic 6 Technique Toolkit**
Bilinearity$\text{Cov}(aX+bY,Z)=a\text{Cov}(X,Z)+b\text{Cov}(Y,Z)$.
Multinomial covariance$\text{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})=-np_{i}p_{j}$ for components of $\mathrm{Mult}(n;\mathbf{p})$.
PSD constraint (3D)$\det(C)\geq0 \Leftrightarrow a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-2abc\leq1$.
Correlation boundsGiven $\text{Corr}(X,Y)=a$ and $\text{Corr}(Y,Z)=b$: $c\in[ab\pm\sqrt{(1-a^{2})(1-b^{2})}]$.
Cholesky construction$Y=\rho X+\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}Z$ (2D). For $n$D: $\mathbf{X}=L\mathbf{Z}$ where $C=LL^{\top}$.
Equicorrelation bound$n$ equicorrelated variables: $\rho\geq-1/(n-1)$.
One-factor model$X_{i}=\sqrt{\rho}F+\sqrt{1-\rho}\varepsilon_{i}$ achieves $\text{Corr}(X_{i},X_{j})=\rho$ for all $i\neq j$.
Order statistics$\mathbb{E}[X_{(k)}]=k/(n+1)$; $X_{(k)}\sim\mathrm{Beta}(k,n-k+1)$.
Cov of min/max$\text{Cov}(X_{(1)},X_{(n)})=1/[(n+1)^{2}(n+2)]$ for Uniform$(0,1)$; positive.
Time-scale paradoxShort-run $\rho>0$ + long-run mean reversion $\Rightarrow$ annual $\rho<0$.

problemIndex.name: Covariance of Face Counts in Repeated Dice Rolls
Correlation Bounds for Three Random Variables
